Squares, absolute values, variances meet important requirement can used safely priors. Adaptive statistics computed dynamically training subjects inside cross-validation split. built-options examine energy (squared amplitude design metric), precision (inverse variance), product. Energy simply ∥K1/2B:α∥22\|K^{1/2} B_{:\alpha}\|_2^2, representing amount signal voxel carries effect space; precision defined 1/(σ2+ε)1/(\sigma^2 + \varepsilon) captures measurement reliability. Combination rules specify prior knowledge adaptive components blended together (product, sum, override operations). mix parameter provides fine-grained control relative balance two sources information. Shrinkage keeps final weights well-behaved applying several regularization steps: winsorize extreme values pull everything back towards 1 ensure single voxel dominates fit. adaptive statistics computed within training fold (enforced scope = "fold" setting). careful isolation preserves validity leave-one-subject-results keeps statistically unbiased.

what: 2. Building weight specifications and fitting DKGE
title: Adaptive Voxel Weighting in DKGE
text: begin exploration default configuration, relies purely adaptive statistics without prior knowledge. Next, create small synthetic dataset work : ten subjects, four effects, sixty voxels per subject. exact numbers unimportant purposes, scale allows us clearly demonstrate API observe effects different weighting strategies. second fit internally computes voxel weights every training fold, applying adaptive weighting strategy specified. can inspect global weights examine average weight values across subjects understand algorithm adjusted influence different voxels. observe modest deviations 1.0 rather extreme spikes weight values. well-behaved pattern exactly shrinkage parameters designed enforce, preventing single voxel dominating analysis.
code: library(dkge)  wts_adapt <- dkge_weights(   prior   = NULL,            # no prior information   adapt   = "kenergy_prec",  # energy × precision, a simple SNR proxy   combine = "product",   mix     = 0.6,             # adaptive term has more influence than the prior   shrink  = list(alpha = 0.5, winsor = 0.99, normalize = "mean"),   scope   = "fold"           # recomputed on the training subjects of each fold )  print(wts_adapt) #> dkge weight specification #>   prior   : uniform  #>   adapt   : kenergy_prec (scope = fold ) #>   combine : product (mix = 0.60 ) #>   shrink  : alpha =0.50, winsor =0.990, normalize =mean make_subject <- function(id, q = 4L, v = 60L) {   design <- diag(q)   colnames(design) <- paste0("eff", seq_len(q))   beta <- matrix(rnorm(q * v), nrow = q)   dkge_subject(beta, design = design, id = paste0("sub", id)) }  subjects <- lapply(seq_len(10L), make_subject) K <- diag(4)  fit_uniform  <- dkge(subjects, K = K, rank = 2, w_method = "none") fit_adaptive <- dkge(subjects, K = K, rank = 2, weights = wts_adapt) head(fit_adaptive$voxel_weights) #> cluster_1 cluster_2 cluster_3 cluster_4 cluster_5 cluster_6  #>     0.904     0.983     0.930     1.236     0.882     0.931 dkge_diagnostics(fit_adaptive)$voxel_weights #> $mean #> [1] 1 #>  #> $sd #> [1] 0.132 #>  #> $min #> [1] 0.862 #>  #> $max #> [1] 1.44

title: Feature-Anchored DKGE
text: vignette describes run DKGE subject observed different set stimuli every item carries feature vector shared space (e.g., 100-dimensional embedding). feature-anchored workflow replaces discrete-cell completion common anchor basis derived directly feature space. : build anchor descriptor subject-specific features item kernels, fit DKGE shared dkge_input interface, evaluate cross-fitted contrasts operate anchor basis.
code: library(dkge) library(Matrix) set.seed(1)

title: Feature-Anchored DKGE
text: example simulate three subjects. subject set item features sampled around four latent prototypes. generate subject-specific beta maps projecting item responses SVD loadings adding gaussian noise.
code: # Number of latent anchors and feature dimension d <- 20L anchors_true <- matrix(rnorm(4 * d), 4, d)  make_subject <- function(n_items, n_vox, seed) {   set.seed(seed)   # Each subject observes item features around the latent anchors   centers <- anchors_true[sample.int(nrow(anchors_true), n_items, replace = TRUE), , drop = FALSE]   features <- centers + matrix(rnorm(n_items * d, sd = 0.4), n_items, d)    # Build an item similarity kernel (e.g., RSA over betas)   latent <- matrix(rnorm(n_items * 3), n_items, 3)   beta_loadings <- matrix(rnorm(3 * n_vox), 3, n_vox)   betas <- latent %*% beta_loadings + matrix(rnorm(n_items * n_vox, sd = 0.2), n_items, n_vox)   item_kernel <- betas %*% t(betas)    list(features = features,        item_kernel = item_kernel) }  subjects <- list(   s1 = make_subject(30, 120, seed = 11),   s2 = make_subject(45, 120, seed = 12),   s3 = make_subject(35, 120, seed = 13) )  features_list <- lapply(subjects, `[[`, "features") K_item_list  <- lapply(subjects, `[[`, "item_kernel")

title: DKGE Architecture Guide
text: dkge_fit() main entry point batch fitting. Today performs long sequence responsibilities one 400+ line function: Coercion: Accept raw lists dkge_data bundles, validate dimensions, attach kernel metadata. Row standardisation: Pool subject designs, compute shared Cholesky factor R, build Btil (R^T B_s). Kernel preparation: Symmetrise kernel, derive square roots/inverses, resolve voxel/anchor weights. Weighting: Compute optional subject MFA weights (svd per subject) voxel weights, accumulating compressed covariance Chat. CPCA branches: Optionally split covariance design/residual parts, re-merge based cpca_part, inject ridge terms. Eigen solve: Run dense eigen() Chat, drop near-zero components, project back K-metric basis. Block assembly: Build concatenated subject matrix, populate multivarious multiblock_biprojector, package diagnostics (weights, contributions, CPCA payloads, cached Chat pieces). structure makes difficult reuse intermediate artefacts (e.g. cached Chat, per-subject contributions) experiment variant solvers, streaming fits, GPU back-ends.

title: DKGE Architecture Guide
text: improve cohesion split lifecycle composable stages explicit inputs outputs: function live R/dkge-fit-core.R (internal) focused unit tests. Public wrappers (dkge_fit(), dkge()) orchestrate stages remain minimal. Key contracts: fit_prepare() returns list containing harmonised data, kernel payload (K, Khalf, Kihalf), Btil, subject ids, resolved weighting specs. fit_accumulate() consumes prepared payload plus weighting options returns Chat, contribs, updated weights, CPCA-ready intermediates. fit_solve() encapsulates cpca_part branching, ridge handling, eigen decomposition, returning basis matrices (U, U_hat), eigenvalues, CPCA splits, ready--project column scalings. fit_assemble() creates multiblock_biprojector, attaches diagnostics, constructs final dkge object. decomposition allows drop-replacement stage (e.g. streaming accumulation, truncated eigen solvers) clarifies test boundaries.
code: +---------------------+    +-------------------+    +--------------------+ | fit_prepare()       | -> | fit_accumulate()   | -> | fit_solve()        | | - coerce inputs     |    | - subject/voxel    |    | - eigen/CPCA       | | - pool designs, R   |    |   weights          |    | - basis assembly   | | - resolve kernel    |    | - compressed Chat  |    | - diagnostics      | +---------------------+    +-------------------+    +--------------------+                                   |                                   v                         +--------------------+                         | fit_assemble()     |                         | - multiblock view  |                         | - scores/loadings  |                         | - result object    |                         +--------------------+

title: Classification with DKGE
text: multiple beta estimates per condition (e.g., separate scanner runs), stack run-specific effects extra rows design matrix supply weight matrix combines single class pattern. plain matrix accepted directly targets — DKGE wraps automatically: cross-validation remains unbiased: run averaging happens inside weight matrix, LOSO fold observes exactly one pattern per condition.
code: # 4 effects: A_run1, A_run2, B_run1, B_run2 make_subject_multi <- function(id) {   design <- diag(4)   colnames(design) <- c("A_run1", "A_run2", "B_run1", "B_run2")    signal <- matrix(0, nrow = 4, ncol = v)   signal[1:2, 1:10] <-  0.5   # both A runs share signal   signal[3:4, 1:10] <- -0.5   # both B runs share the opposite signal    beta <- signal + matrix(rnorm(4 * v, sd = 1.0), nrow = 4)   dkge_subject(beta, design = design, id = paste0("sub", id)) }  subjects_multi <- lapply(seq_len(n_subjects), make_subject_multi) fit_multi      <- dkge(subjects_multi, K = diag(4), rank = 2)  # Weight matrix: rows = classes, columns = effects. # Each class averages its two runs. W_runs <- matrix(0, nrow = 2, ncol = 4) W_runs[1, 1:2] <- 0.5    # class A = mean(A_run1, A_run2) W_runs[2, 3:4] <- 0.5    # class B = mean(B_run1, B_run2) rownames(W_runs) <- c("A", "B") cls_multi <- dkge_classify(fit_multi,                            targets = W_runs,   # plain matrix dispatch                            n_perm  = 99,                            seed    = 101)  res_multi <- cls_multi$results[[1]] res_multi$metrics #>     accuracy      logloss  #> 1.000000e+00 6.953406e-06 as.data.frame(cls_multi) #>    target   metric        value p_value n_perm #> 1 target1 accuracy 1.000000e+00    0.01     99 #> 2 target1  logloss 6.953406e-06    0.01     99

what: When and Why to Use CPCA Filtering
title: CPCA Filtering
text: CPCA filtering becomes valuable experimental design naturally contains multiple types task effects warrant separate analysis. Rather analyzing effects together single latent space, CPCA allows focus analysis specific effect types cleanly separating others. Common scenarios CPCA proves beneficial: Factorial designs: Separate main effects interaction terms understand basic experimental manipulations differ combined effects. example, 2×2 design studying attention working memory, might isolate main effects factor interaction. Experimental versus control conditions: Focus analysis experimental manipulations factoring baseline control conditions. approach can reveal cleaner patterns conditions interest. Multi-domain studies: studying different cognitive processes within experiment, separate effects related different domains (e.g., working memory versus attention) understand domain-specific versus shared neural mechanisms. Planned versus exploratory contrasts: Isolate primary hypotheses exploratory secondary analyses, ensuring main effects interest receive focused statistical attention.

Next, dkge_cpca_split_chat() applies projector split compressed covariance design residual components. Finally, DKGE fits separate bases components request preserving KK-orthogonality returned bases, ensuring can analyzed jointly. can specify design-aligned subspace either naming specific effects cpca_blocks providing explicit basis matrix via cpca_T. cpca_part argument determines filtered components returned ("design", "resid", ""), cpca_ridge optionally adds regularization stabilize small eigenvalues decomposition.

title: CPCA Filtering
text: demonstrate CPCA filtering realistic neuroimaging scenarios, simulate data factorial attention-working memory experiment. design includes two main effects (attention cue validity working memory load), interaction, plus additional control conditions. structure naturally lends CPCA analysis, might want isolate main experimental effects interaction control conditions. simulated dataset contains strong signals primary experimental manipulations (attention working memory main effects) weaker meaningful variance interaction term control conditions. mirrors real neuroimaging studies primary effects interest typically show stronger consistent patterns secondary effects. fit standard DKGE model without CPCA filtering, task-related variance compressed single latent basis. leading eigenvalues reflect combined influence primary experimental effects secondary conditions, making difficult isolate specific patterns want study. eigenvalues represent mixed signal experimental conditions. standard approach captures dominant patterns data, doesn’t allow us focus specifically primary experimental manipulations versus interactions control conditions.
code: S <- 8 q <- 6 P <- 16 Tlen <- 80 effects <- c("attn_valid", "attn_invalid", "wmem_high", "wmem_low", "interact", "control")  betas <- replicate(S, {   # Strong signals for main experimental effects   main_effects <- matrix(rnorm(2 * P, sd = 1.5), 2, P)   # Weaker signals for interaction and control conditions   secondary_effects <- matrix(rnorm((q - 2) * P, sd = 0.4), q - 2, P)   mat <- rbind(main_effects, secondary_effects)   rownames(mat) <- effects   mat }, simplify = FALSE)  designs <- replicate(S, {   X <- matrix(rnorm(Tlen * q), Tlen, q)   X <- qr.Q(qr(X))   colnames(X) <- effects   X }, simplify = FALSE)  subjects <- Map(function(b, X, id) dkge_subject(b, X, id = id),                 betas, designs, paste0("sub", seq_len(S))) bundle <- dkge_data(subjects) fit_plain <- dkge(bundle, K = diag(q), rank = 3) round(fit_plain$evals[1:4], 3) #> [1] 2507 2107  209  180

title: CPCA Filtering

Setting cpca_part = "" instructs DKGE return design-aligned basis (focused primary effects) residual basis (containing secondary effects). dual analysis allows us examine effect types maintaining mathematical independence. Notice CPCA filtering cleanly separated variance components. design eigenvalues now reflect primary experimental effects specified, residual eigenvalues capture secondary effects including interactions control conditions. separation allows focused analysis effect type. mathematical beauty approach lies preservation KK-orthogonality design residual bases. orthogonality ensures two sets components mathematically independent design kernel metric, preventing contamination primary secondary effect patterns:

Reducing to 2 components.  fit_cpca$cpca$part #> [1] "both" round(fit_cpca$cpca$evals_design[1:3], 3) #> [1] 2503 2095    0 round(fit_cpca$cpca$evals_resid[1:3], 3) #> [1] 213 182 157 Ud <- fit_cpca$cpca$U_design Ur <- fit_cpca$cpca$U_resid round(max(abs(t(Ud) %*% fit_cpca$K %*% Ur)), 6) #> [1] 0

code: rho_vals <- seq(0.4, 1.0, by = 0.3) K_grid <- list() for (ra in rho_vals) {   for (rb in rho_vals) {     nm <- sprintf("A%.1f_B%.1f", ra, rb)     K_grid[[nm]] <- design_kernel(       factors,       terms = terms,       rho = c("A" = ra, "B" = rb, "A:B" = 0.4),       basis = "cell",       normalize = "unit_trace"     )$K   } }  cv_rows <- lapply(names(K_grid), function(nm) {   cv <- dkge_cv_rank_loso(bundle$betas, bundle$designs, K_grid[[nm]], ranks = 1:3)   cbind(kernel = nm, cv$table) }) cv_table <- do.call(rbind, cv_rows) head(cv_table) #>      kernel param  mean      se #> 1 A0.4_B0.4     1 0.582 0.02751 #> 2 A0.4_B0.4     2 0.761 0.01993 #> 3 A0.4_B0.4     3 0.935 0.01012 #> 4 A0.4_B0.7     1 0.598 0.03118 #> 5 A0.4_B0.7     2 0.812 0.01427 #> 6 A0.4_B0.7     3 0.948 0.00808 best_idx <- which.max(cv_table$mean) best <- cv_table[best_idx, ] best #>       kernel param  mean      se #> 27 A1.0_B1.0     3 0.968 0.00501  K_best <- K_grid[[best$kernel]] fit <- dkge(bundle, K = K_best, rank = best$param) round(fit$sdev, 3) #> [1] 9.83 5.36 4.94

code: library(dkge) set.seed(2024)  S <- 3        # subjects q <- 3        # effects per design P <- 4        # clusters per subject T <- 30       # time points per subject design  betas <- replicate(S, matrix(rnorm(q * P), q, P), simplify = FALSE) designs <- replicate(S, {   X <- matrix(rnorm(T * q), T, q)   qr.Q(qr(X))  # orthonormalise columns to stabilise the fit }, simplify = FALSE)  centroids <- replicate(S, matrix(runif(P * 3, min = -15, max = 15), P, 3), simplify = FALSE)  subjects <- lapply(seq_len(S), function(s) {   dkge_subject(betas[[s]], design = designs[[s]], id = paste0("sub", s)) })  data_bundle <- dkge_data(subjects) identity_kernel <- diag(data_bundle$q)

title: Design-Kernel Group Embedding for Cluster-Level fMRI
text: Design-Kernel Group Embedding (DKGE) turns subject-level GLM outputs shared, design-aware latent space. preserves structure experimental designs, supports cross-validated contrasts, provides transport utilities mapping parcellated fields onto common anchor voxel representations.

title: Design-Kernel Group Embedding for Cluster-Level fMRI
text: Flexible design kernels – encode factorial structure, smoothness, interactions control effects align across subjects. Robust contrasts inference – LOSO/K-fold cross-fitting, analytic approximations, bootstrap utilities medoid voxel maps. Transport & rendering – barycentric kNN C++-accelerated Sinkhorn mappers warm starts, anchor graph smoothing, voxel decoders. Classifier localisation – cross-fitted latent classifiers decoder, Haufe, LOCO maps bias-aware whole-brain interpretation. Component interpretability – convenience helpers projecting new data, rotating components, summarising variance explained.

title: Build a flexible design-similarity kernel — design_kernel
text: factors Named list factor specifications. factor described list containing: type "nominal" | "ordinal" | "circular" | "continuous" (default "nominal"). L Number levels (discrete types). values Numeric coordinates continuous factors. l Optional length-scale ordinal/circular/continuous factors. terms List character vectors describing factors appear kernel term (e.g. list("","B", c("","B"))). Defaults main effects plus full interaction. rho Named numeric weights per term (names like "", ":B"). Defaults 1 term omitted. Must non-negative. include_intercept Logical; TRUE adds small identity ridge (controlled `rho0`) keep kernel full rank (default TRUE). rho0 Non-negative scalar ridge weight added `include_intercept` TRUE (default 1e-8). basis Either "cell" (kernel design cells) "effect" (kernel regressors/effects). Default "cell". contrasts Optional named list per-factor contrast matrices used `basis="effect"`. Defaults sum--zero contrasts discrete factors single column ones continuous factors. block_structure Optional ordering effect blocks (names matching terms). NULL, uses order `terms`. normalize One "unit_trace", "none", "unit_fro", "max_diag". Controls kernel scaled construction (default "unit_trace"). jitter Small diagonal jitter added `K_cell` numerical stability (default 1e-8).

text: betas Either () list qxP_s beta matrices (one per subject), (ii) list/tibble [dkge_subject()] objects, (iii) pre-built `dkge_data` bundle. Rows must align design effects. designs Optional list T_sxq design matrices subject GLMs. column corresponds effect/regressor; column names become canonical effect labels enforced across subjects. Ignored `betas` already carries `dkge_subject`/`dkge_data` entries. K Design kernel expresses similarity smoothness effects design space (e.g. identity nominal factors, RBF ordinal factors, Kronecker combinations interactions). Supply either qxq numeric matrix list returned [design_kernel()], whose `K` element extracted automatically. Matches `K` parameter name used [dkge_fit()]. Omega_list Optional list overriding per-subject spatial weights. element may `NULL`, length-P_s numeric vector, P_sxP_s matrix. betas voxelwise (e.g. coming `NeuroVec`), weights operate spatial units (voxels) rather clusters; equal weights assumed omitted. Matches `Omega_list` parameter name used [dkge_fit()]. subject_ids Optional subject identifiers used raw matrices provided. Ignored `betas` carries IDs already. keep_inputs Logical; `TRUE` (default) returned object stores canonicalised `dkge_data` bundle `$input` later inspection prediction. cpca_blocks Optional integer vector specifying effect rows span CPCA design subspace. Ignored `cpca_part = "none"` `cpca_T` provided. cpca_T Optional qxq0 matrix giving CPCA design basis explicitly. Overrides `cpca_blocks` supplied. cpca_part CPCA-filtered component fit: `"none"` (default) performs standard DKGE fit; `"design"` uses CPCA design part; `"resid"` uses residual part; `""` fits design part also stores residual basis. cpca_ridge Optional ridge applied CPCA-filtered matrices eigen-decomposition. weights Optional [`dkge_weights()`] specification overrides default adaptive weighting behaviour accumulating covariance. Use inject custom spatial reliabilities reuse precomputed weighting recipe produced [dkge_weights()]. kernel Deprecated. Use `K` instead. omega Deprecated. Use `Omega_list` instead. ... Additional arguments forwarded [dkge_fit()] (e.g. `rank`, `ridge`, `w_method`, `weights`).

title: Fit DKGE across multiple subjects — dkge
text: `dkge` object containing learned basis (`$U`), eigenvalues,   pooled design Cholesky factor (`$R`), compressed covariance matrix, subject weights,   metadata (subject IDs, effect names, cluster identifiers) derived   input bundle.

title: Fit DKGE across multiple subjects — dkge
text: design matrices `X_s` supplied T_sxq regressors   used fit subject-level GLMs; columns must align effects   encoded kernel. kernel `K` captures effects relate one   another-identity recovers standard OLS scaling, structured kernels (e.g.   RBF ordinal factors, circulant wrapped factors, Kronecker products   interactions) encourage shared smoothness coupling design effects. DKGE operates entirely low-dimensional design space: (1)   pooled Gram matrix across subjects yields shared Cholesky factor `R`; (2) beta   matrix row-standardised; (3) compressed covariance accumulated   K-metric optional subject weighting; (4) tiny eigenproblem produces   K-orthonormal group basis. input harmonisation performed   wrapper ensures consistent effect naming, subject identifiers, spatial   weights downstream utilities [dkge_loso_contrast()] can work without   additional bookkeeping. Use [dkge_subject()] build subjects raw   matrices, `NeuroVec`, `ClusteredNeuroVec` sources prior calling `dkge()`.

title: Analytic LOSO contrast using eigenvalue perturbation — dkge_analytic_loso
text: function implements first-order eigenvalue perturbation approximation described paper. held-compressed covariance: Chat^(-s) ~ Chat - w_s S_s eigenvalues eigenvectors updated using: - deltalambda_j = -w_s v_j^T S_s v_j (eigenvalue shift) - deltav_j = -w_s Sum k!=j (v_k^T S_s v_j)/(lambda_j - lambda_k) v_k (eigenvector rotation) avoids O(q^3) eigen-decomposition, requiring O(q^2r) operations r rank. approximation accurate : 1. Subject weights w_s small (single subject dominates) 2. Eigenvalue gaps large (well-separated components) 3. perturbation S_s aligned transition regions conditions violated (detected via condition number eigenvalue gaps), function can fall back full eigen-decomposition.

title: Analytic LOSO contrast using eigenvalue perturbation — dkge_analytic_loso
text: Golub, G. H., & Van Loan, C. F. (2013). Matrix computations (4th ed.). Stewart, G. W., & Sun, J. (1990). Matrix perturbation theory.

title: Compute DKGE contrasts with cross-fitting — dkge_contrast
text: list class `dkge_contrasts` containing:   - `values`: Named list contrast values (one P_s vector per subject per contrast)   - `method`: Cross-fitting method used   - `contrasts`: Input contrast specifications   - `metadata`: Method-specific metadata (fold assignments, bases, etc.)

title: Compute DKGE contrasts with cross-fitting — dkge_contrast
text: function provides unified interface three cross-fitting strategies: 1. **LOSO** (`method = "loso"`): Recomputes basis excluding subject,    projects subject's data. gold standard unbiased    estimation requires S eigen-decompositions. 2. **K-fold** (`method = "kfold"`): Splits data K folds, recomputes basis    excluding fold, projects held-data. efficient LOSO    maintaining good bias properties. Supports time-based, run-based, custom    fold definitions. 3. **Analytic** (`method = "analytic"`): Uses first-order eigenvalue perturbation    theory approximate LOSO solution without full recomputation. Fast    may less accurate subjects high leverage. methods work entirely qxq design space respect K-metric throughout. Multiple contrasts can evaluated simultaneously efficiency.

title: Define folds for K-fold cross-validation — dkge_define_folds
text: Different fold types serve different purposes: **Subject-level folds** (`type = "subject"`): Assigns entire subjects folds. maintains subject independence appropriate subjects exchangeable. fold approximately S/k subjects. **Time-based folds** (`type = "time"`): Splits subject's time series k temporal blocks. Useful assessing temporal stability early vs late responses differ. Requires access original time series dimensions. **Run-based folds** (`type = "run"`): Uses experimental runs natural folds. Common fMRI runs provide natural breaks. Requires run indicators. **Custom folds** (`type = "custom"`): Full control fold assignments. Supply list element specifies indices fold.

code: dkge_fit(   data,   designs = NULL,   K = NULL,   Omega_list = NULL,   w_method = c("mfa_sigma1", "energy", "none"),   w_tau = 0.3,   ridge = 0,   rank = NULL,   keep_X = FALSE,   cpca_blocks = NULL,   cpca_T = NULL,   cpca_part = c("none", "design", "resid", "both"),   cpca_ridge = 0,   weights = NULL,   solver = c("pooled", "jd"),   jd_control = dkge_jd_control(),   jd_mask = NULL,   jd_init = NULL )

title: Fit a Design-Kernel Group Embedding (DKGE) model — dkge_fit
text: data `dkge_data` bundle (preferred) list subject beta matrices. designs Per-subject design matrices (ignored `data` already carries ). Columns must align effects encoded `K`. K qxq design kernel effect space. Supply either raw matrix list returned [design_kernel()]. Omega_list Optional per-subject spatial reliabilities/weights. element may `NULL` (extra weighting), numeric vector length `P_s` (diagonal weights clusters/voxels), full `P_s x P_s` matrix specifying custom covariance. weights applied accumulating compressed covariance computing MFA/energy block normalisation. w_method Subject-level weighting scheme. * `"mfa_sigma1"` (default): inverse squared leading singular value   \(K^{1/2} Btil_s \Omega_s^{1/2}\) (Multiple Factor Analysis scaling). * `"energy"`: inverse Frobenius norm squared block. * `"none"`: disable block scaling (weights = 1). w_tau Shrinkage parameter (0..1) toward equal weights. 0 keeps raw weights, 1 forces equal weighting. ridge Ridge regularization parameter (default 0). rank Desired rank decomposition. NULL, uses full rank. keep_X Logical; `TRUE`, store concatenated training matrix used build multiblock projection (can large). cpca_blocks Optional integer vector specifying effect rows span CPCA design subspace. Ignored `cpca_part = "none"` `cpca_T` provided. cpca_T Optional qxq0 matrix giving CPCA design basis explicitly. supplied overrides `cpca_blocks`. cpca_part CPCA-filtered component estimate: `"none"` (default) performs standard DKGE fit; `"design"` uses CPCA design part; `"resid"` uses residual part; `""` fits design part also stores residual basis inspection. cpca_ridge Optional ridge added CPCA-filtered matrices eigen-decomposition. weights Either `NULL` (default) apply weighting scheme specified `w_method`, [`dkge_weights()`] specification controlling additional voxel/anchor-level weighting. supplied, must inherit `dkge_weights` resolved via [dkge_weights()]. solver Solver used q-space problem. `"pooled"` keeps original eigen-decomposition; `"jd"` performs joint diagonalisation using [dkge_jd_control()] settings. jd_control Control parameters JD solver. jd_mask Optional mask (matrix list matrices) applied -diagonal penalty JD. supplied single matrix recycled across subjects. jd_init Optional orthogonal initialiser JD solver expressed whitened \(K^{1/2}\) metric (qxq matrix).

title: Unified inference for DKGE contrasts — dkge_infer
text: function integrates cross-fitting machinery [dkge_contrast()] various statistical inference procedures. first computes contrast values using specified cross-fitting method, applies chosen inference procedure obtain p-values, finally applies multiple testing correction. subject cluster counts differ across participants, supply `transport` argument contrasts first mapped shared parcellation stacking. resulting mapped subject matrices returned `transport` field output downstream inspection. workflow : 1. Compute contrast values via cross-fitting (LOSO/K-fold/analytic) 2. Apply inference procedure (sign-flip/Freedman-Lane/parametric) 3. Apply multiple testing correction (maxT/FDR/Bonferroni) sign-flip inference, max-T correction provides strong FWER control. parametric inference, FDR may appropriate exploratory analyses.

Z_by_subject Optional list latent cluster features used estimate fold covariances stored `clf`. lambda Non-negative smoothing parameter applied via [dkge_anchor_aggregate()]. to_vox Logical; `TRUE` decoder available `renderer`, dense voxel map produced. inference One `"none"`, `"signflip"`, `"parametric"` specifying group inference routine applied anchor maps.

code: # \donttest{ toy <- dkge_sim_toy(   factors = list(A = list(L = 2), B = list(L = 3)),   active_terms = c("A", "B"), S = 6, P = 20, snr = 5 ) fit <- dkge(toy$B_list, toy$X_list, kernel = toy$K, rank = 2) #> Warning: Argument 'kernel' is deprecated; use 'K' instead. centroids <- lapply(toy$B_list, function(B) matrix(rnorm(ncol(B) * 3), ncol(B), 3)) renderer <- dkge_build_renderer(fit,                                 centroids = centroids,                                 anchor_xyz = matrix(rnorm(20 * 3), 20, 3),                                 anchor_n = 20,                                 anchor_method = "sample") y <- factor(rep(c("class1", "class2"), length.out = length(fit$Btil))) folds <- dkge_define_folds(fit, type = "custom",                            assignments = list(c(1, 2), c(3, 4), c(5, 6))) clf <- dkge_cv_train_latent_classifier(fit, y, folds = folds) enc <- dkge_info_map_haufe(fit, clf, renderer, inference = "none", to_vox = FALSE) length(enc$mean_anchor) #> [1] 20 # }

what: Create a pluggable DKGE anchor mapper for dense rendering — dkge_mapper
title: Create a pluggable DKGE anchor mapper for dense rendering — dkge_mapper
text: Constructs mapper descriptor **dense rendering / anchor pipeline** — used projecting subject-space voxel/parcel values onto set 3-D spatial anchor points (e.g., medoid centroids).  Pass result [dkge_build_renderer()], [dkge_render_subject_values()], directly [fit_mapper()] together `subj_points` / `anchor_points` matrices. Use [dkge_mapper_spec()] instead need **transport pipeline mapper** operates abstract feature space (ridge regression, Sinkhorn OT embeddings) functions [dkge_prepare_transport()] [dkge_transport_spec()].

title: Create a pluggable DKGE anchor mapper for dense rendering — dkge_mapper
text: type Mapper backend identifier: `"knn"` (barycentric kNN, fully implemented), `"sinkhorn"` (OT point clouds), `"ridge"`, `"gw"` (Gromov-Wasserstein; latter three require external plugins). ... Backend-specific parameters stored within mapper object (e.g. `k`, `sigx`, `sigz` kNN; `epsilon` Sinkhorn).

title: Multi-output regression on DKGE effects — dkge_regress

title: Multi-output regression on DKGE effects — dkge_regress
code: dkge_regress(   fit,   Y,   effect_ids = rownames(Y),   folds = NULL,   k = 5,   features = "effects",   engine = "glmnet",   alpha = 0,   standardize = TRUE,   seed = 1,   return_models = FALSE )

title: Multi-output regression on DKGE effects — dkge_regress
text: fit `dkge` object containing effect-space basis (`fit$U`). Y Numeric matrix shape `(#effects) x (#outputs)` row names identify effects/items modelled. effect_ids Optional character vector effect identifiers include (defaults `rownames(Y)`). folds Optional list giving held-effect IDs fold. `NULL`, random K-fold splits generated. k Number folds create `folds` `NULL` (default `5`). features Either string "effects" (use fitted DKGE effect embeddings) custom function `function(fit, ids, ctx)` returning matrix predictors supplied effect IDs. function may use optional `ctx` argument receive fold-specific context objects. engine Regression engine. One "glmnet" (default ridge multi-output regression), "lm" (ordinary least squares solved jointly across outputs), list `fit`/`predict` closures: `list(fit = function(X, Y) {...}, predict = function(model, X) {...})`. alpha Elastic-net mixing parameter passed glmnet (0 ridge, 1 lasso). standardize Logical; `TRUE` (default) glmnet engine standardises predictors internally. seed Random seed used generating folds. return_models Logical; `TRUE`, attach fitted model fold returned object.

title: Multi-output regression on DKGE effects — dkge_regress
text: object class `"dkge_regress"` containing predictions (`pred`),   observed targets (`truth`), fold assignments (`folds`), summary metrics   (`metrics`), optionally per-fold models (`models`).

title: Multi-output regression on DKGE effects — dkge_regress
code: # \donttest{ set.seed(1) Q <- 4L effects <- paste0("e", seq_len(Q)) betas <- replicate(3, matrix(rnorm(Q * 6), Q, 6, dimnames = list(effects, NULL)), simplify = FALSE) designs <- replicate(3, diag(Q), simplify = FALSE) designs <- lapply(designs, function(X) { colnames(X) <- effects; X }) fit <- dkge_fit(betas, designs = designs, K = diag(Q)) W <- matrix(rnorm(ncol(fit$U) * 2L), ncol = 2L) rownames(W) <- colnames(fit$U) Y <- fit$U %*% W rownames(Y) <- effects res <- dkge_regress(fit, Y, k = 2, engine = "lm") print(res) #> dkge_regress results #>   RMSE (micro): 1.6879 #>   R^2  (micro): -6.3174 #>   R^2  (macro): -6.2059 #>   Cosine mean : -0.2194 # }

title: Simulate toy DKGE datasets with known factorial structure — dkge_sim_toy
text: Generates subject-level beta matrices \(B_s\) (`q x P_s`) effect space user-selected active design terms, controlled signal--noise ratio (SNR), per-subject noise levels. routine also returns per-subject design 'rulers' (typically identity matrices) design kernel \(K\) synthetic dataset can fed directly [dkge_fit()]. Components planted inside chosen effect blocks, \(K\)-orthonormalised provide ground truth subspace.

title: Simulate toy DKGE datasets with known factorial structure — dkge_sim_toy
code: dkge_sim_toy(   factors,   terms = NULL,   active_terms,   r_per_term = NULL,   S = 3,   P = 10,   snr = 8,   noise_scales = NULL,   seed = 1L,   contrasts_type = c("helmert", "sum") )

title: Simulate toy DKGE datasets with known factorial structure — dkge_sim_toy
text: factors Named list describing experimental factors ([design_kernel()]), e.g. `list(= list(L = 2), B = list(L = 3))`. terms Optional list character vectors specifying terms include kernel. Defaults full factorial set used [design_kernel()]. active_terms Character vector term names activate (e.g., `c("", "B", ":B")`). must present kernel. r_per_term Named integer vector giving many latent columns draw within active term. Defaults one column per term. S Number subjects. P Either single integer (clusters/voxels per subject) length-`S` integer vector. snr Target Frobenius SNR (signal / noise) per subject. Scalar length-`S`. noise_scales Optional length-`S` multipliers applied noise standard deviation subject. seed RNG seed reproducibility. contrasts_type Factor-contrast system, one `"helmert"` `"sum"`.

title: Simulate toy DKGE datasets with known factorial structure — dkge_sim_toy
text: list following entries: B_list List `q x P_s` beta matrices. X_list List subject-level design rulers (identity matrices). K `q x q` design kernel. info Design metadata returned [design_kernel()]. U_true Ground-truth `q x r_true` component basis (\(K\)-orthonormal). M_list Per-subject spatial patterns (`P_s x r_true`). active_cols Named list mapping active term selected       column indices effect basis. subject_ids Character vector subject identifiers.

title: Simulate toy DKGE datasets with known factorial structure — dkge_sim_toy
text: Construction follows model $$B_s = U_{true} M_s^\top + E_s,$$ `U_true` lies inside requested design-term blocks, `M_s` encodes subject-specific spatial loadings, `E_s` Gaussian noise adjusted hit requested Frobenius SNR.

title: Simulate toy DKGE datasets with known factorial structure — dkge_sim_toy

title: Create a DKGE voxel-weight specification — dkge_weights
text: prior Optional prior weights: numeric vector length V, logical mask, integer indices, ROI labels (factor/character/integer length V) per voxel. Helpers [dkge_weights_prior_mask()] [dkge_weights_prior_roi()] ease construction. adapt Adaptive weighting rule, one `"none"`, `"kenergy"`, `"precision"`, `"kenergy_prec"`. combine prior adaptive sources combine: `"product"` (default), `"sum"`, `"override_adapt"`, `"prefer_prior"`. mix Numeric [0,1] controlling relative influence adaptive component. Interpreted log-space `combine = "product"`. shrink List fields `alpha` (shrink towards uniform), `winsor` (upper quantile cap), `normalize` (`"mean"` `"sum"`), optional `roi_smooth = TRUE` median-smooth within ROIs. scope Either `"fold"` (default: compute adapt weights training subjects within fold) `"subject"` (per-subject adaptive weights averaged fold pooling). k_weight Optional effect-space kernel k-energy rules. `NULL` reuse kernel stored fit, optional factor `collapse`. collapse Optional list describing factor collapses (e.g., `list(time = "mean")` `list(time = list(method = "mean", window = 3:8))`). roi Optional ROI labels used `shrink$roi_smooth = TRUE`.

title: Create a DKGE voxel-weight specification — dkge_weights
text: Object class `"dkge_weights"`.

title: Create a DKGE voxel-weight specification — dkge_weights
text: `"product"` Geometric mean prior adaptive weights, blended     log-space `mix` (default). Smooth robust. `"sum"` Linear mixture: `(1 - mix) * prior + mix * adaptive`. `"override_adapt"` Adaptive weight used wherever finite     positive; falls back prior otherwise. Adaptive takes priority. `"prefer_prior"` Prior weight used wherever finite     positive; falls back adaptive otherwise. Prior takes priority.
